name: Cobalt
name_origin: From the German 'Kobold' meaning 'goblin' or 'evil spirit'. Medieval miners gave the name to ores that yielded toxic arsenic-containing fumes when smelted and that resembled silver and copper ores but produced no useful metal.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Cobalt is the 33rd most abundant element in Earth's crust (~25 ppm) and is rarely found in pure form. It is mined chiefly as a byproduct of copper and nickel production; the Democratic Republic of the Congo supplies more than half of the world's cobalt.
states: byproduct of copper and nickel mining
essential trace element in vitamin B12 (cobalamin)

compounds.name: Cobalt(II) oxide
compounds.summary: An olive-green solid used as a precursor to other Co compounds and to impart blue colors to glasses and ceramics.

compounds.name: Cobalt(II,III) oxide
compounds.summary: A black mixed-valence oxide used as a battery cathode component and in ceramic pigments.

compounds.name: Cobalt(II) chloride
compounds.summary: A blue (anhydrous) or pink (hydrated) salt used as a humidity indicator and as a precursor in cobalt chemistry.

compounds.name: Lithium cobalt oxide
compounds.summary: The original and still common cathode material in rechargeable lithium-ion batteries for portable electronics.

compounds.name: Cobalamin (vitamin B12)
compounds.summary: An essential vitamin built around a cobalt-containing corrin ring; required for red blood cell formation and nerve function.

compounds.name: Cobalt aluminate (cobalt blue)
compounds.summary: A vivid blue pigment used in ceramics, glass, and high-quality paints since antiquity.

uses: Cathode materials in lithium-ion batteries (LiCoO2, NMC, NCA chemistries)
Superalloys for jet engine and gas turbine hot-section components
Cobalt-bonded tungsten carbide (cemented carbide) for cutting and drilling tools
Permanent magnets, including SmCo and Alnico alloys
Cobalt blue and cobalt green pigments in ceramics, glass, and art
Radioactive 60Co as a gamma-ray source for cancer radiotherapy and industrial radiography
reactivity_summary: Cobalt is a hard, lustrous, silvery-white ferromagnetic metal with a Curie temperature of 1388 K - the highest of any element. It is relatively unreactive in air at room temperature, slowly oxidizing on heating. Cobalt dissolves slowly in dilute mineral acids and is passivated by concentrated nitric acid. The +2 and +3 oxidation states are most common, with +3 stabilized in low-spin octahedral complexes.
isotopes_summary: Cobalt has only one stable isotope, 59Co, making it monoisotopic. The radioisotope 60Co (half-life 5.27 years) is widely used in radiotherapy, food sterilization, and industrial radiography; 57Co is used as a Mössbauer source.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Cobalt
